title: 5 Common Pitfalls in Cloud Data Migrations And How to Avoid Them

children.text: 5 Common Pitfalls in Cloud Data Migrations And How to Avoid Them

Cloud adoption is no longer a question of "if", it's a matter of "when." But while cloud data migration offers a path to increased scalability, reduced costs, and improved performance, the journey isn't always smooth.

expecting a plug-and-play experience, only to hit roadblocks that delay projects, drive up costs, or even cause data loss. The good news? Most of these issues are entirely avoidable with the right strategy.
Cloud Data Migrations : Challenges and Solutions
Here are five of the most common pitfalls in cloud data migration projects, and how your organization can steer clear of them.
1. Failing to Assess Data Before Migration

One of the biggest mistakes companies make is migrating everything without understanding what they actually have. Unused datasets, outdated applications, and duplicated information get moved, creating clutter and inflating cloud costs.

before you begin your cloud data migration. Identify what’s mission-critical, what’s redundant, and what can be archived or deleted. Data classification and cleanup will save time, reduce risk, and make your cloud environment easier to manage long-term.
Not all data belongs in the same kind of cloud architecture. Some workloads thrive in object storage within a
, while others require the performance and structure of a
. Choosing the wrong model can lead to sluggish performance and limited usability.

Design your architecture based on your business needs, not just tech trends. Consider hybrid models that combine data lakes and warehouses, and use services like Amazon Redshift, Azure Synapse, or Google BigQuery for optimized outcomes. Engage with experts to architect a solution tailored to your data types, user needs, and analytics goals.

Security isn’t just an IT issue, it’s a business-critical concern. During a cloud data migration, sensitive information can be exposed if not properly encrypted, logged, or monitored. Industries like healthcare, finance, and government face especially steep penalties for compliance breaches.

Build security into the cloud migration process from day one. That means encrypting data in transit and at rest, managing access controls, and maintaining compliance with regulations like HIPAA, GDPR, or CCPA. Use cloud-native tools to monitor activity and establish audit trails.

Data migration often slows to a crawl because teams underestimate the bandwidth needed or don’t plan for downtime. This leads to missed deadlines and frustrated users when systems go offline longer than expected.

Assess your current network infrastructure and build a detailed migration timeline. Consider using phased migrations or hybrid cloud setups to minimize disruptions. If you’re moving large volumes of data, explore physical data transfer services like AWS Snowball or Google Transfer Appliance to reduce latency issues.

Too many businesses think the job is done once the data is in the cloud. But if it’s not optimized, indexed, structured, and integrated into business processes, then you’ve simply traded on-prem clutter for cloud clutter.

Plan for performance tuning, cost optimization, and user training after the migration. Set up dashboards, implement data governance tools, and ensure your teams know how to access and use the new environment.

title: Demystifying Data Lakes vs. Data Warehouses in the Cloud

children.text: Demystifying Data Lakes vs. Data Warehouses in the Cloud

Many organizations are generating and collecting more data than ever before, and knowing how to store, access, and use that data effectively is crucial. Two powerful storage architectures dominate the conversation:
But what’s the difference, and how do you know which one is right for your business?
In this article, we’re breaking down the key differences between these two cloud-based data solutions, when to use each, and how
can help you build a smarter data foundation.

A data lake is a centralized storage repository that allows you to store all of your structured and unstructured data at any scale. That means everything, from documents and images to sensor data, logs, and raw database exports, can be stored in one place without needing to define its structure upfront.
Think of a data lake as a massive digital filing cabinet where data can be dumped “as-is.” Data scientists and engineers can then process and analyze that data using big data tools,
Store raw, unprocessed data at scale
Highly flexible for analytics, AI, and ML
Ideal for IoT, clickstream, social media, and log data

A data warehouse, on the other hand, is a structured system optimized for reporting and business intelligence. It stores cleaned, processed, and organized data that has been transformed into a consistent schema.

Data warehouses are ideal for structured data that needs to be queried efficiently. They’re perfect for generating reports, dashboards, and KPIs across departments like finance, sales, and operations.

When considering data lakes vs. data warehouses, it often comes down to your goals, users, and tools. Many modern enterprises actually use both in a hybrid architecture, creating a pipeline where raw data lands in a data lake, is transformed, and then loaded into a warehouse for analysis.

title: The DBA’s Role in a Cloud World

children.text: The DBA’s Role in a Cloud World
The cloud hasn’t replaced the DBA—it’s made them more critical than ever.
The cloud has changed the way businesses manage, store, and access their data, but one thing that hasn’t changed is the importance of the
. While the traditional on-prem environment may be giving way to hybrid and cloud-native architectures, the DBA remains as critical as ever, just with a modernized set of responsibilities.

Traditionally, a DBA was the gatekeeper of all things database: backups, patches, performance tuning, and access control. But in the cloud, many of these tasks are automated or handled by the platform. Does that mean the DBA’s role is disappearing? Absolutely not. Instead, it’s evolving.

Today’s cloud-savvy DBA must take on a more strategic role, optimizing data storage, integrating cloud services, supporting DevOps, and ensuring compliance across distributed environments. The modern DBA helps companies design cloud architectures, scale resources dynamically, and prepare data for advanced analytics or AI/ML workloads.
DBA Responsibilities in the Cloud Era
Cloud platforms like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud offer powerful tools—but they also introduce complexity. DBAs must adapt to:
Modern organizations are rarely 100% in one cloud. The DBA must manage data across on-prem, cloud, and hybrid environments, ensuring availability, consistency, and performance.
Just because a database is hosted in the cloud doesn’t mean it performs optimally out of the box. DBAs now tune resources like compute, memory, and IOPS in cloud-native tools like
, Azure SQL Database, or Google Cloud SQL to improve application speed and reliability.

The cloud introduces usage-based pricing models. An inefficient query or over-provisioned database can cause unnecessary spend. DBAs now play a key role in right-sizing infrastructure and managing cloud costs through performance monitoring and automation.

DBAs are on the front lines of ensuring data is encrypted, access is controlled, and compliance standards are met—whether it’s HIPAA, SOC 2, or GDPR. In cloud environments, shared responsibility models mean the DBA must deeply understand what the provider secures and what the business must secure on its own.

DBAs today work more closely with developers than ever before. With the rise of infrastructure as code and CI/CD pipelines, cloud DBAs create automated deployment scripts, manage containerized databases, and ensure that data is accessible without compromising integrity or performance.
Why DBAs Are More Valuable Than Ever

Organizations are relying on their DBAs to help them make smarter decisions about their data strategy, select the right tools and platforms, and support digital transformation initiatives. Cloud environments demand faster response times, smarter integrations, and real-time insights, and DBAs are essential to delivering on those promises.
As cloud adoption accelerates, the role of the DBA will continue to evolve in exciting and strategic ways:
to proactively identify and prevent performance or security issues—before they impact users.
As data becomes more distributed across global environments, DBAs will play a critical role in ensuring compliance, standardization, and integrity.
DBAs will partner more closely with finance teams to monitor usage, control cloud spend, and deliver database environments that balance performance with cost-efficiency.

Why AI for JDE Is a Game-Changer

Traditionally, JDE environments required manual intervention for many everyday tasks, whether it was reviewing logs, managing workflows, or digging through documents for key data. But with the power of AI for JDE, those time-consuming processes are being replaced with intelligent automation and real-time insights.

AI solutions sit alongside JDE orchestrations and integrate seamlessly into your environment, without disrupting core functionality. Whether you’re running JDE on-premise or in the cloud, AI tools can be added to improve operational efficiency and user experience.
AI-Powered Features That Are Transforming JDE
Let’s explore some of the specific AI features that are transforming how companies use JDE:
Imagine a world where your users can ask a question like, “What’s the status of PO

?” and get an immediate answer, without digging through screens or reports.  With integrated AI chat, JDE users can interact with the system using natural language, improving productivity and reducing reliance on IT or help desk tickets.

Say goodbye to manually entering or scanning PDFs and Excel files. Errors in your data or processes can cost time and money. JDE orchestrations are powerful, but pairing them with AI supercharges what they can do. For example, an AI model can trigger a JDE orchestration when specific conditions are met, analyze the result, and then determine the next best action. This creates a closed-loop system where decision-making is partially or fully automated, cutting down on cycle time and reducing human error.

title: Relational Databases Are Still at the Heart of Modern Data Architectures

children.text: Relational Databases Are Still at the Heart of Modern Data Architectures
Every so often, the tech world gets caught up in absolute declarations. A keynote speaker, a conference post, or a vendor blog will make bold claims like: “
are dead” or “DBAs will be obsolete within ten years.” Those speeches feel powerful in the moment, but the truth is, their shelf life often ends before the applause dies down.
The latest example? Vendors who spent years telling us to abandon relational databases in favor of data lakes and modern architectures are now reintroducing them under new names.
Databricks now sells “Lakebase,” Snowflake has “Snowflake Postgres,” and Microsoft offers “
” Despite the new branding, these are still the same tried-and-true relational databases that have powered businesses for decades.

, we’ve seen this cycle play out before. Predictions about the end of SQL and the obsolescence of DBAs resurface every few years, but when the dust settles, organizations always come back to the fundamentals: relational databases, strong governance, and expert database management.
Years ago, some industry leaders insisted that SQL databases had no place in modern architecture. Relational
systems, they argued, were relics destined to fade, with data lakes and distributed platforms taking their place.

Fast forward, and those same vendors are now building solutions that bring relational databases back to the center of the architecture. Why? Because relational databases continue to do what they’ve always done best: provide structured, reliable, and governed data management.

The truth is that technology always evolves. New platforms, programming languages, and database tools emerge to solve specific problems. Some gain traction, others fade away, and a few reshape the landscape. But the infrastructure, both logical and physical, that businesses have built around relational databases means they’ll remain the backbone of enterprise systems for decades to come.
Data Lakes, Warehouses, and Relational Databases: A Balanced View
That’s not to say relational databases are the answer to everything. In fact, part of the industry’s challenge has been shoehorning systems into relational databases that never belonged there.

are excellent for storing unstructured data at scale. Data warehouses excel at large-scale analytics across structured datasets. And relational databases provide the guardrails, governance, and transactional power needed for day-to-day business operations.
The best architectures don’t reject one for the other, they strike the right balance. Relational databases remain central, with lakes and warehouses supporting them where appropriate.

have been declared obsolete. Around 2005, many industry articles predicted that improvements in automation would eliminate the DBA role within a decade. Yet here we are, twenty years later, with DBAs still at the center of database strategy and governance.
The lesson is clear: hype cycles will come and go, but foundational technologies endure. Relational databases, and the professionals who manage them, continue to prove their value year after year.

At Main Street, we’ve built our reputation on guiding clients through these hype cycles while keeping their most critical systems secure and optimized. Our team blends modern tools with the proven reliability of relational databases, ensuring that your systems are high-performing, compliant, and ready for growth.

We don’t just manage databases, we help you architect a balanced data environment that leverages the strengths of relational databases, warehouses, and lakes together. With our AI-enabled monitoring, governance expertise, and 25+ years of DBA experience, we deliver stability in a landscape that’s always changing.
Back to the Basics, With a Modern Edge
Relational databases are not relics. They are the foundation of secure, efficient, and governed data architectures. No matter what new label vendors put on them, the core principles remain unchanged.

title: MariaDB vs. MySQL: Which Database is Right for Your Business?

children.text: MariaDB vs. MySQL: Which Database is Right for Your Business?

When it comes to relational databases, MySQL and MariaDB are two of the most widely known database management systems. While they share a common history, there are key differences that can impact performance, scalability, and security.

If you're deciding between MariaDB vs. MySQL understanding their strengths and trade-offs is essential for choosing the right database solution for your business. Let’s dive into the core differences and explore which database best fits your needs.

MySQL is one of the most popular relational database management systems (RDBMS) in the world. Originally developed by MySQL AB in 1995, it was later acquired by Sun Microsystems and then by Oracle Corporation in 2010.

MySQL is widely used in enterprise applications, websites, and cloud services due to its stability, security, and compatibility with a wide range of platforms. Major companies, including Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube, rely on MySQL for their backend data operations.

MariaDB was developed as a fork of MySQL in 2009 by the original MySQL developers after Oracle acquired MySQL. Designed to remain open-source and community-driven, MariaDB maintains compatibility with MySQL while introducing performance improvements and new features.
MariaDB has quickly become a preferred database for businesses looking for enhanced speed, scalability, and flexibility.

title: Building a Scalable Data Governance Framework

children.text: Building a Scalable Data Governance Framework
Organizations must treat their data as one of their most valuable assets. As businesses scale and generate more
, ensuring this information is governed, compliant, and secure is essential. That’s where data governance comes in. At
, we understand that a robust data governance framework is the key to unlocking operational efficiency and long-term business success.

We’ll explore why data governance is critical for IT success, how it drives growth, and the challenges businesses must overcome to implement effective governance strategies. We’ll also discuss how tools and
support can help you build a scalable, future-proof data governance framework that ensures compliance and drives business performance.
Why Data Governance Is Critical for IT Success

As organizations adopt more digital tools and generate vast amounts of data, managing and protecting this data becomes more challenging. A well-defined data governance framework establishes the policies, processes, and standards that ensure data is accurate, secure, and accessible. Without a proper governance structure, businesses risk making decisions based on incorrect or incomplete data, potentially leading to costly mistakes.

Data governance is about more than risk mitigation— creating value through effective data management. By implementing a strong framework, businesses can harness the full potential of their data, improve decision-making, and drive growth. Whether you're working with customer data, financial information, or operational metrics, having a clear governance policy can transform raw data into actionable insights.
The Role of Data Compliance in Enterprise Growth

Data compliance is a cornerstone of data governance in an era of ever-increasing regulatory scrutiny. From GDPR in Europe to CCPA in California, businesses are subject to a growing number of data protection laws. Non-compliance can lead to hefty fines, reputational damage, and even legal ramifications. A scalable data governance framework ensures that your organization stays compliant with these regulations, safeguarding your

Compliance is not just about avoiding penalties—it's about building trust with your customers and stakeholders. A strong commitment to data compliance demonstrates that your organization takes data privacy and security seriously, enhancing customer loyalty and driving enterprise growth.
How Governance Improves Decision-Making Across Departments

Effective data governance also plays a critical role in improving decision-making. With clear policies, departments can access trusted, accurate data when needed, leading to more informed decisions. Whether it's marketing relying on customer insights, finance analyzing revenue trends, or operations streamlining processes, having consistent, high-quality data enables better cross-departmental collaboration.
A scalable data governance framework fosters a culture of d
. When teams can trust the data they’re working with, they’re empowered to make faster, more informed choices contributing to the organization’s overall goals.
Boosting Efficiency by Reducing Manual Processes

can be time-consuming, error-prone, and inefficient. A well-structured data governance framework automates many data-related processes, improving workflow efficiency. By defining who owns the data, who is responsible for its accuracy, and how it’s shared, businesses can reduce the need for manual intervention and mitigate the risk of human error.

Automation also enables teams to focus on higher-value tasks, such as analyzing data to drive strategic initiatives. Organizations can boost productivity, reduce operational costs, and improve overall performance with streamlined data governance processes.

While the benefits of data governance are clear, implementing a scalable framework is not without its challenges. Here, we’ll take a closer look at some common hurdles businesses face in data governance and how to overcome them.
Addressing Data Silos to Enable Seamless Flow
One of the most significant challenges in data governance is the existence of

Data is often stored across different departments, applications, and systems as businesses grow, leading to fragmentation. This siloed data structure makes accessing and managing information difficult, limiting the potential for data-driven decision-making.

is crucial for ensuring that all teams have access to the same high-quality data. Businesses can enable seamless data flow and collaboration across departments by implementing a data governance framework that centralizes data storage, standardizing data formats, and creating shared access protocols.
Ensuring Accurate and Secure Data Migration Processes

Data migration is another critical aspect of data governance, particularly for businesses undergoing digital transformation or integrating new systems. Data accuracy and security during migration are essential to avoid
or loss. Without proper governance, data migration can result in inconsistent data, compliance risks, and security vulnerabilities.

A scalable data governance framework should include clear policies for data migration, such as data validation procedures, encryption protocols, and data backup strategies. These policies will ensure that your data remains secure and accurate during the migration process, reducing the risk of errors or breaches.
Fostering Team-Wide Buy-In for Governance Policies

One of the biggest challenges in implementing data governance is ensuring that all employees across the organization are aligned with governance policies. Without buy-in from stakeholders at all levels, governance initiatives can quickly fall flat. It's essential to communicate the importance of data governance and the role that each team member plays in maintaining data quality and security.

Fostering a culture of data stewardship is key. This involves training employees on the importance of data compliance and governance policies and creating an environment where team members are accountable for the data they manage.
Tools and Expertise to Ensure Long-Term Compliance

to developing customized governance policies, our team is here to guide you through the process.

But if that data is unreliable, outdated, or inaccurate, the consequences can be costly—ranging from operational inefficiencies to missed business opportunities.

That's why organizations can’t afford to overlook data integrity. With the right IT advisory services, businesses can identify, address, and overcome data integrity issues, ensuring their data remains a powerful asset, not a liability.

Data integrity refers to the accuracy, consistency, and reliability of data throughout its lifecycle. While modern technologies promise vast amounts of data to fuel business growth, many organizations still struggle to maintain the integrity of that data. Let’s explore some common challenges leading to

One of the biggest culprits of data integrity issues is outdated IT systems and infrastructure. Many organizations continue to rely on legacy systems that were never designed to handle today’s volume and complexity of data. These systems often lack the modern capabilities needed to validate, process, and store data accurately.

) systems might not integrate seamlessly with newer applications or databases, causing gaps in data flow and inconsistencies. Without timely updates and improvements, these systems may produce incomplete or erroneous data, which leads to poor decision-making.

Data entry is another key area where integrity issues often arise. Whether it’s manual data input, inconsistent formatting, or errors during data migration, entering data into systems can introduce inaccuracies. Even the smallest mistake—like entering a customer’s address incorrectly—can result in incorrect records, inefficiencies, and missed opportunities.

because they lack strong data governance policies. Data governance involves creating clear rules and guidelines for collecting, storing, and using data across the organization. Without proper governance, data can become fragmented, mismanaged, or misused, leading to integrity issues.

Organizations can easily fall into the trap of using inaccurate or incomplete data in their operations without established procedures for handling data—such as regular audits, validation checks, and data quality controls. A lack of oversight can also make maintaining consistency and standardization across departments and systems difficult.

title: The Benefits of a Hybrid Cloud Environment

children.text: The Benefits of a Hybrid Cloud Environment
Many organizations are increasingly looking for ways to enhance their
IT infrastructure’s efficiency, flexibility, and scalability.
The hybrid cloud environment has emerged as one of the most compelling solutions, offering the best of both worlds: the security and control of a private cloud, combined with the scalability and
Key Factors to Consider When Transitioning to Hybrid Cloud Environments
As a leader, consider these key points when deciding if a hybrid solution is right for your organization.

One of the primary advantages of a hybrid cloud is its ability to dynamically scale resources based on demand. Businesses can run critical workloads on their private cloud for better control and data privacy, while leveraging the public cloud to handle surges in traffic or unexpected demands.

A retail company can use its private cloud to manage regular, day-to-day operations but expand into the public cloud during high-traffic periods, such as Black Friday or holiday seasons, without investing in additional permanent infrastructure.
A hybrid cloud enables organizations to

by running predictable workloads in a private cloud, where resource costs are fixed, and scaling into the public cloud when necessary, where businesses pay only for what they use. This pay-as-you-go model of the public cloud reduces upfront capital expenditures while ensuring businesses have the capacity they need during peak times.
An enterprise can move less critical or seasonal workloads to the public cloud, reducing the need for expensive on-premises hardware.

For organizations in highly regulated industries, such as healthcare or finance, maintaining data security and compliance is paramount. A hybrid cloud allows companies to keep sensitive data on-premises or in a private cloud, where they have full control over security protocols, while utilizing the public cloud for non-sensitive operations.

A financial institution can store confidential customer data in a private cloud while using the public cloud to run analytics on anonymized or non-sensitive data, ensuring compliance with regulations such as GDPR or HIPAA.

Hybrid cloud environments offer an excellent solution for business continuity and disaster recovery (DR) strategies. Organizations can replicate their critical data and applications across both private and public clouds, ensuring redundancy in the event of an outage, disaster, or cyberattack.
If an on-premises data center experiences a failure, a company can seamlessly shift operations to a public cloud, minimizing downtime and ensuring uninterrupted service for customers.

Hybrid cloud environments enable organizations to embrace cutting-edge technologies like AI, machine learning, and big data analytics without requiring a complete overhaul of their existing infrastructure. Businesses can use
to experiment with new technologies and scale resources quickly, while continuing to run critical operations on their private infrastructure.

A healthcare organization can use AI-powered analytics tools available in the public cloud to analyze patient data and improve care outcomes while ensuring that sensitive personal data remains secure within the private cloud.
Hybrid cloud environments give organizations the flexibility to choose from
, avoiding dependency on a single vendor. This multi-cloud approach provides leverage in negotiating costs and allows businesses to use the best solution for each specific workload.
A business can use one cloud provider for data storage and another for analytics, optimizing performance and cost while maintaining the ability to switch providers if necessary.

cost efficiency, and security. It is an ideal choice for organizations looking to optimize their
, manage workloads efficiently, and remain competitive in today’s evolving technological landscape.

By combining the strengths of both private and public cloud environments, a hybrid cloud strategy can drive innovation, improve business agility, and ultimately provide a more resilient and future-proof infrastructure.

title: Features within JD Edwards Server Manager

children.text: Features within JD Edwards Server Manager
is a web-based toolset which provides a central location to manage and review the overall
The tool simplifies many procedures for key administration tasks like JDE recycling, quickly access log information, and review/adjust configuration settings.

In addition, it also monitors many of the internal components of EnterpriseOne.  Configuration allows email alerts to be sent to the right people to ensure timely responses to issues that may arise.  This innovation in system management was key to making administration teams more efficient and effective.  In
the cost savings of utilizing Server Manager grew exponentially as the system and hardware footprint grew.
Why JD Edwards Server Manager is Better Than Third Party Tools

Early versions of Server Manager were not as robust in functionality as it provides today.  Monitoring was limited, metrics only provided 24 hours of data, and the toolset provided no automation.  Because of these limitations, we saw third-party variants hit the market offering to pick up the slack. Some third party tools duplicated certain features of

, enhanced some functionality, and even provided additional controls or monitoring.  Many of these tools directly connect to the EnterpriseOne database for information, while others gather data by hijacking communications with the Server Manager agents in the system.  Given the architecture of these tools, they become problematic when a new tools releases is introduced.
Disadvantages with Third Party Reporting Tools

Third party tools often have limits around functionality, based on which application and tools version of EnterpriseOne is running.  This is typically due to constraints around R&D efforts.  These tools are often built and developed with one or two application versions of EnterpriseOne.   Since multiple tools releases per year are common, each release can drastically alter functionality of a third party tool.  A minor update has the potential to break or alter the desired functionality which renders the tool useless until the vendor provides an update.  Weeks or months of testing is required with every new Tools Release and its

combination.  Does it require retrofitting the code or redesign of the code to work with the new version?  Will that change allow it to still function with the previous version? These are questions which a third party tool designer must ask at every release of tools and application change to EnterpriseOne.

team at Oracle listened to the feedback from the community and continued to enhance Server Manager.  As iterations of the Server Manager tool were released the team at Oracle enhanced existing features and introduced new game changing features like REST API calls for integration with Orchestrator. The improvements to this core administration tool continue to be expanded with each release.
Server Manager in the JD Edwards Release 9.2.5
With the release of Server Manager in the 9.2.5x tools released in late 2020 we can see two key feature enhancements:

1.) Kernel failure alerting functionality, which will expedite troubleshooting of kernel issues.  The system will automatically identify the kernel failures, capture the related log files along with the problem call stack, and send an email notification.  This automated process can be configured by the administrator to determine who receives notifications.  The notification contains all the contextual information, which helps to reduce the time spent in resolving issues and working with Oracle Support. This enables quicker response so that corrective action is performed promptly.

2.) Automated TLS Configuration between Server Manager Console and Agents.  This feature simplifies and automates the configuration of Transport Layer Security (TLS) based communications between Server Manager Console and Agents.  Providing secure communications between the applications across a network by enabling message encryption, data integrity, and authentication.  Allowing administrators to easily implement secure communications within the

These two new feature examples show there is a huge advantage to sticking with Server Manager as your central administrations tool of choice.  Oracle's ability and knowledge to integrate kernel failure alerting via Server Manager is beyond anything that any third parties can do within reasonable time and overhead considerations.  Features like TLS security that help protect the data may cause certain features on third party tools to stop functioning.

monitoring and management toolset is compatible, secure, and always supported by Oracle.

title: Protecting Your Business from Data Security Threats

children.text: Protecting Your Business from Data Security Threats
Data security is no longer optional—it’s essential. A single breach can lead to costly financial losses, damage to your reputation, and legal repercussions.

That’s why Main Street offers powerful, comprehensive data security solutions designed to safeguard your organization and ensure peace of mind. Explore how we can help fortify your defenses and protect what matters most.
Early Detection and Response: Continuous Security Monitoring
Continuous monitoring is critical for information security and risk management.

At Main Street, real-time detection and quick responses to security threats are essential. Our InfoVault practice involves continuously analyzing and identifying potential security threats so we can respond promptly and mitigate risks. This proactive approach helps manage security risks and ensures compliance with regulatory requirements.

Think of continuous monitoring as your digital security guard, keeping a vigilant eye on your environment, detecting real-time risks, and responding quickly to potential threats. Implementing these measures, we help businesses avoid potential breaches, minimizing operational and financial impacts.
Protecting Data In Transit and At Rest: Encryption, Access Control, and Strong Authentication

Encryption, access control, and strong authentication are fundamental strategies we use at Main Street to protect data in transit and at rest. Encrypting data at rest secures files and documents, ensuring only those with the key can access them. This prevents data leakage, unauthorized access, and physical theft. Encrypting data in transit using secure protocols like SSL/TLS ensures that data remains protected as it moves between users, databases, and applications.

Access control mechanisms, including authentication and authorization systems, effectively manage user access and actions. Role-based access control (RBAC) ensures that users only have access to the data necessary for their roles, reducing the risk of unauthorized access. Strong authentication methods add an extra layer of security, ensuring that only authorized users can access sensitive information.

These measures provide numerous benefits to businesses, including enhanced data protection, reduced risk of breaches, and increased customer trust. By safeguarding sensitive information, businesses can maintain their reputation and avoid data breaches' financial and legal repercussions.
Regular maintenance, including software updates, patching, and security audits, is crucial for maintaining a secure database environment.

At Main Street, we ensure these practices are consistently applied to address vulnerabilities before malicious actors can exploit them. Software updates often include patches that fix vulnerabilities or bugs that hackers can exploit to access systems or data. By installing the latest updates, businesses can reduce the risk of cyberattacks and protect personal and business information.

Conducting regular security audits helps identify potential security gaps and ensures that all systems comply with the latest security standards. This proactive approach to security helps businesses stay ahead of threats, safeguarding sensitive data and maintaining system integrity against an increasingly sophisticated digital threat landscape.

At Main Street, we keep servers secure by regularly applying security patches and setting up automated tools for timely updates. Staying informed on security alerts and conducting periodic audits to identify and fix vulnerabilities are essential practices we follow.

title: Sync Slicers in Power BI

children.text: We previously talked about using toggles/bookmarks in our blog
Hidden Treasures in Power BI: A Baker’s Dozen

(#13).  The toggle solution is ideal when end-users prefer to keep everything on one page, save on real estate by reducing the clutter, and want the slicers to apply across all related toggled-to visuals.
With Sync Slicers, however, we can quickly attain a similar result and provide more detail if desired, across multiple pages assuming two key points:
1.) Users are not averse to moving through multiple pages in Power BI, and
2.) It is desired that any slicer selection should automatically apply to all visuals
using the same slicer among all other pages.
In Power BI, select your slicer and then, from the top of the screen, select View. Then from the Show Panes ribbon, Sync Slicers:

We can see that the slicer is on both the first page, “Yearly Trend” and on the second page, “Products”, and is set to Visible. If it were not visible yet on the other pages, that may be selected here as well – under the visible/eyeball icon.

Again, the Sync Slicers option is used when we have multiple pages in Power BI. By default, when a slicer is changed in one page of visuals, it is not changed on any of the other pages. That very well may be the desired effect. So, we use Sync Slicers when we want ALL pages to be updated regardless of which page we change the slicer selection.
The Sync option is to the right of “Page name” and looks like a recycle/rotation icon. Select the pages where the Slicer will sync. In this case we only have two pages and we will select both:
Now when we select our slicer values on one page, whether it be the 1st or 2nd page in this example, it carries through to the other pages using this slicer:

Reciprocally, if the slicer is changed on Page 2, the “sync” will automatically update Page 1 as well. This feature also works when using multiple slicers and we can even go a step further using Advanced Options to create groups to sync slicers using different fields.
Let us know if you’d like to learn more about syncing multiple filters or syncing Grouped slicers.

title: Virtual Batch Queues in JD Edwards 9.2.5

children.text: Virtual Batch Queues in JD Edwards 9.2.5

With Tools Release 9.2.5.x on E1 9.2, JD Edwards now has the option to enable and configure Virtual Batch Queues (VBQ). Batch processes (UBE and Reports) are a fundamental aspect of a healthy Enterprise One system. With the implementation of the new VBQ feature JD Edwards now adds many key enhancements around flexibility and elasticity to the system.

Once VBQ has been setup, it acts as a Queue Management system before jobs are sent to a specific server. The system allows us to define multiple servers to a virtual queue and configure that virtual queue to be the destination for jobs. If we setup three batch servers within a virtual queue and send a job to that queue, the system will determine which server should execute it. For example, if this virtual queue is thirty wide (10 for each server) and we have jobs waiting, the next job in line for that queue will go to the first server in the cluster that becomes open.

The queue width is also flexible based on configuration of the VBQ. So, we can adjust based on server resources for each server in a virtual cluster. You may have a VBQ defined to run across three batch servers, although one of those has less RAM installed. So, we configure the two larger servers to run 10 wide each and the third, smaller server to only be 5 wide. The result will provide a VBQ which can process 25 jobs concurrently across the three servers.

A critical factor that was required for this to be successful is around single threaded queues and the ability to retain the functionality it provides. The new VBQ system does manage to retain the power of single threaded queues, and now allows us to manage it across multiple servers. We can define a VBQ as single threaded within the system, so Enterprise One will ensure that jobs run in order even if that queue spans across multiple servers.

Another key aspect addresses an issue that has challenged users around failed jobs due to simple data issues. Functionality has been included to provide the ability to resubmit a job which ended in error, without having to re-enter data selection and processing options. This will save time and frustration for end users working within the system.
System administrators will be excited about VBQ for multiple reasons. The flexibility that this feature offers will allow more options in supporting a live production system.

·  System maintenance will be more flexible as we can pull a server out of the mix to do maintenance and not hold up jobs from processing. They will just process on the other servers defined within the VBQs.
·  The ability to re-run bulk jobs in error due to a hardware failure. With the ability to move or resubmit jobs between servers and/or job queues, we can recover much faster than before.

·  Provide flexibility to utilize compute power of the entire system to manage processing jobs will help balance the resources across the system. Even if this is just to provide some additional compute power for month-end/year-end activities, so normal day activities are not impacted.

Some things to consider around VBQ as of the 9.2.5.1 release. This functionality is only supported on Windows, Linux and UNIX platforms which are support by Enterprise One 9.2 technical requirements. Due to the native queue management design on the iSeries, VBQ is not supported on that platform currently.

Server Congruence is also a factor for servers that will be grouped into a VBQ virtual host. They need to be configured identical in most aspects. Such as Operating System, E1 port, DB type, OS time zone, and printer definitions to name a few. The VBQ feature also requires that Batch storage in the Database be enabled. Which will centralize all the reporting output for easy retrieval and management.

VBQ is another great tool which enhances JD Edwards and show how it continues to improve as we move forward. This will be very helpful for current systems and allows for many new options in the future of JD Edwards.
